specific_change: Downsize the primary RDS instance from db.r6g.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32 GiB RAM) to db.r6g.large (2 vCPU, 16 GiB RAM) and enable storage auto-scaling on the 200 GB volume. This halves the database compute cost, saving approximately $1,680/month (50% reduction). Update the Terraform resource aws_db_instance.database_primary by setting instance_class to "db.r6g.large" and adding max_allocated_storage = 500 to enable storage auto-scaling.

conclusion.headline: Downsize db.r6g.xlarge → db.r6g.large and enable storage auto-scaling; save 50%

telemetry_observations: db_query_p95_latency_ms p95 is 80.9ms — well under the 800ms SLA target.
db_connections_p95 peaks at 35, with a mean of 26 — far below xlarge connection limits.
db_io_wait_p95 peaks at 4.0ms with a mean of 2.36ms — negligible I/O pressure.
db_cache_hit_ratio stays above 95.6% (mean 97.4%) — buffer pool is oversized for the workload.
All metrics show low stddev, indicating flat, stable utilization across the full 14-day window.
infrastructure_context: Primary RDS instance is db.r6g.xlarge (4 vCPU, 32 GiB RAM) at $3,360/month.
Storage is manually provisioned at 200 GB with no auto-scaling configured (no max_allocated_storage).

reasoning: Every utilization signal — query latency, connection count, I/O wait, and cache hit ratio — is flat and low across the entire 14-day window, indicating the db.r6g.xlarge instance is substantially over-provisioned. The p95 query latency of 80.9ms leaves a 10× margin to the 800ms SLA, and peak connections of 35 are well within the limits of a db.r6g.large. Downsizing to db.r6g.large halves the vCPU and memory, which the workload clearly does not need, while enabling storage auto-scaling removes the operational risk of manual storage management as data grows.

projected_state.notes: With half the memory, buffer pool will be smaller but cache hit ratio should remain above 95% given the modest working set. Latency may increase slightly but will stay far below the 800ms SLA threshold.

cost_impact.notes: db.r6g.xlarge on-demand is ~$0.4608/hr × 730 hrs ≈ $336.38/mo per instance; with 1 replica the total is ~$3,360/mo. db.r6g.large is ~$0.2304/hr × 730 hrs ≈ $168.19/mo per instance; total ~$1,680/mo. Savings = $1,680/mo. Storage costs remain unchanged.

risk_assessment.primary_risk: Unexpected workload spikes (e.g., batch analytics jobs or seasonal traffic) could push the smaller instance closer to resource limits, potentially increasing query latency.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Set CloudWatch alarms on CPUUtilization > 70%, FreeableMemory < 2 GiB, and DatabaseConnections > 80. Monitor db_query_p95_latency_ms for two weeks post-change. Enable Enhanced Monitoring at 15-second granularity during the transition period.
risk_assessment.rollback: Modify the RDS instance class back to db.r6g.xlarge via Terraform apply. The change requires a brief maintenance window (~5-10 min downtime) but is fully reversible with no data impact.
risk_assessment.notes: Schedule the instance modification during a low-traffic maintenance window (e.g., Sunday 04:00-06:00 UTC) to minimize user impact from the required reboot.
